title: One-Hot Encoding: Unordered Categories
concept: One-hot encoding converts unordered categories into multiple binary columns, where a single 1 represents the active category.

exercises.prompt: Warmup: If we encode 'Small', 'Medium', and 'Large' as 0, 1, and 2, which encoding technique did we use?
exercises.explain: Label (or ordinal) encoding maps ordered categories directly to numbers to preserve their hierarchy.

cards.title: Red, Blue, or Green?
cards.body: If a car is 'Red', it isn't 'greater' or 'less' than a 'Blue' car. Since colors have no natural order, mapping them to 1, 2, and 3 is misleading.

cards.title: One-Hot Encoding
cards.body: One-hot encoding creates a new binary (0 or 1) column for every unique category. For each row, only the column of the active category gets a 1 ('hot'), and the others get a 0 ('cold').

cards.title: The light switch panel
cards.body: Imagine a panel of switches, one for each color. If the car is red, you flip the 'Red' switch ON (1) and leave the 'Blue' and 'Green' switches OFF (0).

cards.title: Animal classifier
cards.body: In a pet database, a 'Species' column with 'Dog', 'Cat', and 'Bird' becomes three columns: is_dog, is_cat, and is_bird. A cat row is represented as: is_dog=0, is_cat=1, is_bird=0.

cards.title: Not a cure-all
cards.body: While one-hot encoding is great for keeping categories independent, it's not perfect. If you have 500 unique categories, it creates 500 new columns, making your dataset massive and sparse.

cards.title: From column to matrix
cards.body: How a single column splits into binary columns:

exercises.prompt: Why is one-hot encoding preferred over label encoding for unordered categories like 'Country'?

options.text: Because it avoids implying a mathematical order or ranking between countries
Because it reduces the number of columns in the dataset
Because computers cannot store numbers larger than 1

exercises.explain: Label encoding would imply that Country 3 is 'greater' than Country 1, whereas one-hot encoding keeps them completely separate and equal.

exercises.prompt: True or false: One-hot encoding a column with 4 unique categories will create 4 new binary columns.
exercises.explain: True. Each unique category gets its own yes/no (binary) column.

exercises.sentence: One-hot encoding represents active categories with the number ___.
exercises.explain: The active category is set to 1 (hot) while all other category columns are set to 0 (cold).

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is the correct one-hot encoding for a 'Blue' item in a dataset of [Red, Blue, Green]?
exercises.explain: Only the is_blue column should be 1 (hot), with the other columns set to 0.

exercises.prompt: You are encoding a column named pet_type with options: 'Dog', 'Cat', 'Fish', and 'Rabbit'. If you use one-hot encoding, what does a row with a 'Fish' look like?
exercises.explain: Correct! Only the column representing 'Fish' has a value of 1, indicating the presence of that category. Next up: how having all these binary columns can sometimes backfire!
